food_subgroup: Stir-fried meat with rice — Thai holy basil style

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai holy basil stir-fry with dual protein over rice. FOODON_00002809 (stir-fried food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Thai basil stir-fry with chicken and beef over jasmine rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific Thai composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving consists of approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed jasmine rice and 250 g of Thai holy basil stir-fry with chicken and beef slices.

nutrient_source.title: Pad Krapao (Thai holy basil stir-fry with minced meat) and steamed jasmine rice — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked jasmine rice, stir-fried chicken, stir-fried beef, and Thai basil; proportions weighted to a 450 g composite serving (200 g rice, 250 g stir-fry).
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Thai dish. Values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked white rice (FDC 168878), cooked chicken breast stir-fried (FDC 171477), cooked beef (lean, pan-fried, FDC 174032), and Thai basil (FDC 172232). Sauce contributions (fish sauce, oyster sauce, soy sauce, oil) are incorporated using standard Thai basil stir-fry recipe proportions from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data as a cross-reference. The composite per-100 g values reflect the full plated dish at the stated serving weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (jasmine, cooked) GI reference; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite Thai basil stir-fry with chicken and beef over jasmine rice. The GI of 68 is derived as an analogous estimate anchored to cooked jasmine white rice (GI ~68–72, University of Sydney GI Database), which constitutes the dominant glycemic carbohydrate component (~200 g of 450 g serving). The protein and fat content of the stir-fry component moderates the overall glycemic response slightly; the composite GI is conservatively estimated at 68 (medium). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (68 × 83.25 g carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 ≈ 56.6 / 100 × serving carbohydrate = 68 × (18.5 × 4.5) / 100 = 68 × 83.25 / 100 ≈ 56.6; however GL is capped at the per-serving calculation: carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g/100g × 450 g = 83.25 g; GL = 68 × 83.25 / 100 = 56.6. Note: GL per serving reported as 56.6 rounded to 56.6 in the field.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1890 mg per standard 450 g serving), which approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single meal; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or consuming a smaller portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by jasmine rice; moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish as the jasmine rice base contributes a medium GI of approximately 68 and a high glycemic load per serving (~56.6 g), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction, substitution with brown rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables may help attenuate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from beef and cooking oil; high sodium from condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to its combined saturated fat content (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g, ~5.4 g per serving) from beef and stir-fry oil, and its high sodium load from fish sauce and oyster sauce, both of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes; choosing leaner protein options and requesting reduced sauce would improve the cardiovascular risk profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Basil Stir-Fry with Chicken and Beef over Jasmine Rice (known in Thai as กะเพราคู่ ราดข้าว, Kaphrao Khu Rat Khao) is a popular Thai restaurant dish that combines two proteins — sliced chicken breast and sliced beef — stir-fried together with fresh Thai holy basil (kraphao), garlic, and fresh bird's eye chillies in a savoury sauce of fish sauce, oyster sauce, and light soy sauce, served over a generous portion of steamed jasmine rice. The dish is a 'double delight' variant of the classic Pad Krapao (ผัดกะเพรา), one of Thailand's most beloved street-food and restaurant staples. The stir-fry is cooked at high heat in a wok, producing a fragrant, slightly charred aroma characteristic of Thai wok cooking (wok hei). The flavour profile is bold, spicy, savoury, and aromatic, with the distinctive anise-like fragrance of Thai holy basil cutting through the richness of the meat and sauce. The texture contrasts tender meat slices with the fluffy, slightly sticky jasmine rice. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a meaningful protein contribution (~9.2 g/100 g) from the dual-protein stir-fry component, moderate carbohydrate from jasmine rice (~18.5 g/100 g), and moderate fat (~4.1 g/100 g). The dish is high in sodium (~420 mg/100 g) due to the condiment-heavy sauce, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The jasmine rice base gives the dish a medium glycemic index (~68) and a high glycemic load per serving, which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Thai restaurants and food courts, including chains such as Saap Saap Thai, and is a popular lunch and dinner option.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite Thai restaurant dish (dual-protein Thai holy basil stir-fry over jasmine rice). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked jasmine rice, stir-fried chicken, stir-fried beef, Thai basil, and standard Thai condiment proportions cross-referenced against FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to jasmine rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates; no single approved-source record exists for this exact composite dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if a direct HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry becomes available for Pad Krapao with dual protein over rice. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component entries used for this estimation. 3. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty. 4. Sodium content is estimated and may vary significantly between restaurants depending on sauce quantities used; the value of 420 mg/100 g reflects a typical restaurant preparation. 5. The halal status is marked false as oyster sauce and the source of the beef are not confirmed halal-certified at the specific restaurant (Saap Saap Thai - Funan); this should be verified with the restaurant directly. 6. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as: GI (68) × carbohydrate per serving (18.5 g/100 g × 450 g = 83.25 g) / 100 = 56.6; this is a high GL per serving. The field glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 56.6. 7. The food_group has been reclassified from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Mixed' as this is a composite dish containing both a grain component (jasmine rice) and a protein-dominant stir-fry component. 8. cuisine_tags from input ('Pad Thai', 'Thai Noodle') are not applicable to this dish and have been excluded; this dish is a Thai basil stir-fry, not a noodle dish.
